text: This video is of a laparoscopic repair of a traumatic diaphragmatic hernia. The patient is a fifty year old male with a traumatic diaphragmatic hernia and a past medical history significant for hereditary hemorrhagic telangiectasias with subsequent development of severe pulmonary hypertension and symptomatic right-sided heart failure. The patient had a penetrating knife wound of the left flank thirty years prior with progressive symptoms of dyspnea and chest pain with recurrent pleural effusions requiring thoracentesis. His pulmonary hypertension was optimized with medical therapy and after cardiac anesthesia clearance he was consented for a laparoscopic diaphragmatic hernia repair.

A preoperative CT scan reveals a left lateral diaphragmatic defect with incarcerated omentum and collapse of the left lower lobe. The patient is positioned in the right lateral decubitus position in preparation for the laparoscopic hernia repair.

An 11mm port is placed in the left upper quadrant for the camera, along with three working ports along the left costal margin. The laparoscopic view reveals the left diaphragmatic defect containing the majority of the patient's omentum.

The surgeon identifies and divides the peritoneal lining along the lateral rim of the diaphragmatic defect using sharp dissection or cautery. After sufficient mobilization, the hernia contents are gently reduced with steady downward traction, utilizing a hand over hand technique.
The surgeon uses cautery to enlarge the diaphragmatic defect with a small neck, allowing the significant hernia contents to be easily reduced back into the abdomen.
The surgeon explores the left chest laparoscopically, carefully detaching any remaining adhesions from the pulmonary parenchyma to prevent post-operative air leaks.

The surgeon uses a suction irrigation device to remove bloody loculated effusions from the incarcerated hernia contents. The remaining left pleural effusion is suctioned. The left lower lobe, previously collapsed, is shown re-expanding as the left lung is preferentially ventilated using a double lumen tube.

The surgeon uses cautery to detach the final attachments of the omentum from the posterior diaphragm. The diaphragmatic defect, measuring approximately three to four centimeters, is surveyed and closed primarily using a laparoscopic suturing device with nonabsorbable suture in a running fashion. The two tails of the suture are tied in the middle, and additional interrupted sutures are placed to ensure adequate closure.

After the defect closure, the surgeon desufflates the abdomen and places a 28 French chest tube. The video shows the patient being extubated post-surgery and the chest tube being removed on the third post-operative day, with no signs of recurrent effusion and improved symptoms.
